coredata.dc:title: Comprehensive reuse of pyrolysis chars from coals for fabrication of highly insulating building materials
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Tengyao
Liu, Sijia
AlMutawa, Fayez
Tanner, Jennifer E.
Tan, Gang
coredata.dc:description: A comprehensive concept for coal conversion is proposed in this work, from which a series of products generated including light volatiles, liquid oils, and pyrolysis chars. The result indicates the optimal temperature for the pyrolysis from organic solvent extraction residue is between 700 °C to 900 °C, producing largest amount of liquid oils and giving rise to a chemically stable pyrolysis char sample with considerable BET specific surface area. The pyrolysis chars are then utilized as an alternative material for the fabrication of heat-insulating building material (char clay plaster-CCP, char brick-CB and foam glass-FA). Due to the inherent large specific surface area and ample pore channels, the water could be absorbed and stored in these pores, resulting in the effects of regulating indoor humidity and also low thermal conductivity. The char-based building material makes the exterior walls good breathing properties, able to maintain a proper humidity level. The building energy saving will be benefited from the excellent insulation property of these building products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal conversion
Pyrolysis chars
Building materials
Porous materials
Thermal properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Concept for comprehensive reuse of pyrolysis char for building materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic processing chart for the comprehensive reuse of char in the fabrication of insulating building materials.
GCMS spectrum (a) and FTIR spectrum (b) of liquid oil samples (inset: FTIR spectrum of ethanol).
(a) Schematic illustration of the synthesizing process. (b) SEM image of coal ash; (c) XRD pattern of coal ash; (d) Optical photos of the foam glass: 2 wt % Na2CO3 and 4 wt % Na2CO3.
(a) Yields of pyrolysis char sample; (b) Thermogravimetric analysis for CER sample.
(a) XRD pattern of CER sample and its pyrolysis char; (b) FTIR spectra of CER sample and its pyrolysis char.
BET specific surface area of CER sample and its pyrolysis char.

(a) Enhanced water capacity of char plaster specimens compared to that of blank plaster; (b) Thermal conductivity performance of plaster specimens; (c) Thermal conductivity variation of specimen as a function of temperature; (b) Specific heat capacity variation of specimen as a function of temperature.
Relationship between thermal conductivity and bulk density of CCP (a) and CB (b) specimens; Relationship between compressive strength and bulk density of CB (c) specimens.

(a) Compressive strength of char brick specimens; (b) Thermal conductivity performance of char brick specimens; (c) Thermal conductivity variation of specimen as a function of temperature; (d) Specific heat capacity variation of specimen as a function of temperature.
List of innovative applications of coal and coal-derived materials.

(Li et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Song et al., 2006; Tao et al., 2017)
(Alcañiz-Monge et al., 1997; Hamada et al., 1988; Song et al., 2016)
(Kumar et al., 2015; Rogers and Plucinski, 2003; Wang et al., 2016b; Xu et al., 2018)
(Dai et al., 2016; Franus et al., 2015; Kose Mutlu et al., 2018; Phuoc et al., 2016; Ponou et al., 2016; Taggart et al., 2018)
(Hu et al., 2014, 2016; Li et al., 2017; Meng et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2013)
This work, (Wang et al., 2016a; Zhu et al., 2016)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Major compounds in pyrolysis oil identified from NIST library.
Analysis of ash compositions in CER samples by ICP-AES.
Ultimate and proximate analysis of coal extraction residue and pyrolysis char samples.
Specific surface area, bulk density, thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity of char plaster samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: * the bulk density is calculated from the mass/volume ratio of the samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical and mechanical properties of char brick specimens.

row.entry: Silica gel granules (Serhat Başpınar and Kahraman, 2011)
Silica fume; fly ash (Demirboğa and Gül, 2003)

head.ce:title: Comprehensive reuse of pyrolysis chars from coals for fabrication of highly insulating building materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Architectural Engineering, University of Wyoming, 1000 E. University Avenue, Dept. 3295, Laramie, WY, 82071, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Architectural Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A comprehensive concept for coal conversion is proposed in this work, from which a series of products generated including light volatiles, liquid oils, and pyrolysis chars. The result indicates the optimal temperature for the pyrolysis from organic solvent extraction residue is between 700 °C to 900 °C, producing largest amount of liquid oils and giving rise to a chemically stable pyrolysis char sample with considerable BET specific surface area. The pyrolysis chars are then utilized as an alternative material for the fabrication of heat-insulating building material (char clay plaster-CCP, char brick-CB and foam glass-FA). Due to the inherent large specific surface area and ample pore channels, the water could be absorbed and stored in these pores, resulting in the effects of regulating indoor humidity and also low thermal conductivity. The char-based building material makes the exterior walls good breathing properties, able to maintain a proper humidity level. The building energy saving will be benefited from the excellent insulation property of these building products.

Schematic illustration of pyrolysis char derived building materials.
Image 1004

ce:section.ce:para: Coal, consisting of various contents with different reaction characteristics, is a limited and non-renewable source on earth. Although there was a drop in coal production and coal consumption in 2016 in the United States due to the rise of renewable energy, coal is still playing an important role for industrial demand and everyday living (Kathe et al., 2018). In particular, the state of Wyoming is a leading coal-producer and provides about 40% of US coal supply (Godby et al., 2015). Direct combustion of coal through fired power station not only wastes hydrogen-rich volatile matter but also give rise to serious pollution (Jia et al., 2016). Carbon dioxide as a greenhouse gas, which is largely originated from combustion of coal (Andrić et al., 2015), has been identified for triggering the global environmental issues including climate change. Aside from carbon element, sulfur and nitrogen in coals can also be released to contribute for smog and acid rain. Therefore, the use of fossil fuels such as coal in its raw form does not meet industrial clean production requirement nowadays.

The refinery of coal generating valuable components including synthesis gas, liquid oil and char has been extensively studied (Gokon et al., 2015; Irfan et al., 2011; Murakami et al., 2015; Mushtaq et al., 2014; Quan and Gao, 2016; Zeng et al., 2017). Coal resource, as a carbon rich solid, performs as a potential starting material for higher valuable products via conversion process. A staged coal conversion utilization technology has been investigated earlier involving the pyrolysis of coal to produce volatile and pyrolysis char, followed by the separation of volatile for pyrolysis gas and tar (Zhang et al., 2015). The tar is an important source of aromatic compounds such as phenol, benzene and toluene. A so-called flash hydropyrolysis has then been explored to increase the tar yield during coal conversion (Miura, 2000). The pyrolysis gas consisting of H2, CO and hydrocarbon is used for fuel production or chemical synthesis (Li and Fan, 2008). The pyrolysis char is used for combustion or further gasification. Numerous literature have studied on the utilization of solid chars that occupy ≥50 wt % of the total weight of coals. As with pristine coals, the pyrolysis char can be combusted to provide heat (Liu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018), or used to improve the fertility of the soil and protect against soil-borne diseases so that it has been utilized as soil amendment (Glaser et al., 2002; Steiner et al., 2008). The gasification of pyrolysis char is another impressive approach to further extract the organics from char solid. Effects of alkali metal salts on the steam gasification of pyrolysis char have been explored, demonstrating K2CO3 had the greatest catalytic activity and the mass loading and temperature affected the production of fuels such as CO, H2 and CH4 (Veraa and Bell, 1978). Later, it has been found that high yield of H2 is generated with low formation of CO and no formation of CH4 as the temperature ranging from 700 to 750 °C at the K2CO3 loading of 10%–17% (Wang et al., 2009). Some research projects had focused on the formation mechanism of fuels and kinetics study during the gasification of pyrolysis char to understand the rate and selectivity of the reaction (Wang et al., 2010; Wen, 1980; Zhang et al., 2010). Nevertheless, the environmental impacts will still not be eliminated through the combustion, gasification or pyrolysis of chars especially for fossil-derived CO2 emissions. Owing to unique properties such as considerable specific surface area and pore channel of pyrolysis char, activated carbon could be prepared by physical or chemical activation but a demineralization process should be undertaken since the ash has little contribution on the porosity of activated carbon (Chen et al., 2015; Teng et al., 1998). In coal refinery, the particle size, porosity and density of the resulting char can be benefited from swelling of a coal under high temperature.

Recent increases in requirements for energy saving and reduced energy consumption in building material could be addressed through the development of novel insulation materials. It is reported that buildings consume up 33% of total energy consumption; and half of that energy is lost through building envelopes (Sutcu and Akkurt, 2009). Thus reducing thermal conductivity in building facades such as walls is a natural choice to decreasing energy consumption in structures (Cha et al., 2012), meaning a brick with low thermal conductivity is preferable because heat loss will be reduced by enhancing insulation properties of the buildings. The method of reducing the thermal conductivity of building material is to reduce the bulk density through creating the pores on the building materials. A related research has investigated the correlation between the thermal conductivity of the building material and its compositional, physical, and microstructural features, demonstrating that open porosity is predominant in improving the thermal property of the building materials (Dondi et al., 2004). A wide range of porous materials or materials having ability of creating pores have been utilized for the fabrication of heat-insulating building materials such as polyurethane foam (Gutiérrez-González et al., 2012), silica gel granules (Serhat Başpınar and Kahraman, 2011), recycled paper (Sutcu and Akkurt, 2009), alumina foam (Shimizu et al., 2013), and expanded polystyrene foam (Bicer and Kar, 2017). Amongst these methods, however, it is worthwhile to note that chemical means of creating pores, involving high temperature swelling or highly-purified reagents, may not be cost effective.

The conventional application routes of pyrolysis chars would not be environmentally friendly or of economic benefit. For instance, direction combustion of pyrolysis char will discharge CO2 and other air pollutants, leading to severe environmental issues. Whereas pyrolysis or gasification of chars will leave a significant amount of fixed carbons in solid residue, that needs to be properly disposed or re-used. In this work, the pyrolysis char has been utilized as an alternative starting material for the fabrication of building materials. The as-fabricated building materials demonstrate not only enhanced insulation properties, but also moisture buffering feature potentially for the humidity regulation in indoor environment. These are predominantly taking advantage of the inherent porosity nature of the building materials as the incorporation of pyrolysis char and there is no chemical pore-creating/swelling procedure involved. According to the evaluation of CO2 footprint using a quantification of CO2 emission and mass balance approach (Andrić et al., 2015; Mays et al., 2009), the carbons in pyrolysis char are entirely captured and performed as insulating building materials, leading to this coal conversion process satisfying industrial clean production requirement. At the end of the life, the building materials could be recycled for soil amendment rather than landfilling. Thus, the environmental issues caused by the carbon dioxide emission could be eliminated through the deposition of carbons in buildings.

Pyrolysis char preparation: Because the physical properties and chemical compositions of a coal vary widely, thus affecting conversion process dramatically, all coal samples in this work comes from Powder River Basin Coal field, WY. The coal extraction residue is extracted with tetralin at 360 °C and 250 psi for 3 h and denoted as CER thereafter. The flow rate of the tetralin is 0.1 mL/min for each gram of dried coal. After being pyrolyzed under nitrogen at elevated temperatures from 500 °C to 900 °C, CER samples were converted to pyrolysis char samples and it is denoted by its pyrolysis temperature.

Char clay plaster (CCP) fabrication: Initially the clay and water are mixed to make a slurry. After 24 h, sufficient char, sand, and water are added to produce a mousse-like plaster. An appropriate mold is selected to prepare cubic plaster samples that can be analyzed by instruments. Three different ratios (40 wt %, 50 wt %, 60 wt % to the original weight of sands) of pyrolysis char added into mixtures are utilized for the preparation of CCP. The weight ratio of clay remains constant (20%). Due to the water absorption feature of pyrolysis char, more water is needed for char-based specimen compared to blank specimen. The weight ratio of water to aggregate (a total weight of sands and pyrolysis char) is 0.37 for blank specimen, whereas the ratio increases to 0.50, 0.56, and 0.62 as the percentage of pyrolysis char is 40 wt %, 50 wt %, and 60 wt %, respectively.

Char brick (CB) fabrication: The fabrication of char brick starts with thoroughly combining all the dry ingredients together including cement, sand, and pyrolysis char. The mass ratio of sands replaced by pyrolysis char is set at 30 wt %, 40 wt %, and 50 wt %. Water is then added until the damp mixture reaches the right consistency. The weight ratio of water and aggregate for blank brick specimen is 0.17. When 30 wt %, 40 wt %, and 50 wt % of sands in CB specimen are replaced by pyrolysis char, this value increases to 0.35, 0.45, and 0.55, respectively. The mixture is then loaded into a mold, followed by the curing process in a controlled laboratory environment at room temperature. Two weeks after leaving the mold, bricks are obtained for further characterization.

Foam glass fabrication: Waste glass and coal ash are used as main starting materials. Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) and NaH2PO4 are chosen as foaming agent and foaming stabilizer, respectively. First, the waste glass which is pulverized by a planetary ball mill (through 100 sieve mesh). Subsequently, 0–80 wt % of waste glass powders and 20–40 wt % of coal ash, together with 2–5 wt % of Na2CO3, 2–3 wt % of NaH2PO4 and water is mixed using mechanical stirring, and then the foam glass specimen with dimensions of 50*40*20 mm are formed, which are dried at 150 °C for 4 h in the oven. The specimen is then put into a conventional chamber furnace and a green body is foamed at 850 °C for 30 min with a heating rate of 3 °C/min. Finally, foam glass is obtained through cooling down in the furnace at 5 °C/min to room temperature. All the heat treatments are conducted under atmospheric pressure.

Pyrolysis char samples: X-ray diffraction (XRD) is conducted by a Rigaku Smartlab diffractometer by using Cu Kα radiation source, operated at 40 kV and 40 mA with an angle of reflection, 2θ, varied between 10 and 90°. BET specific surface area of the samples is measured by a Quantachrome Autosorb IQ automated gas sorption analyzer. TG analysis is conducted in CAMCOR center, University of Oregon. Organic elemental composition (C, H, N, O and S) in the liquid tars is characterized by a Vario MACRO Cube Elemental Analyzer (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra are recorded on a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) using attenuated total reflectance (ATR) technique. FTIR spectra are obtained by collecting 32 scans at a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the measuring range of 4000–400 cm−1 wavenumbers. The elemental composition is analyzed by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES; Thermo).

Building materials: The moisture buffering test follows the ISO standard (ISO, 2008), and ASTM standard (Rode et al., 2007). Use climatic exposures which vary in 24 h cycles: 24 h of high humidity (75%) followed by 24 h of low humidity (40%). The plaster specimens are sealed with aluminum foils on all exteriors but one surface to ensure vapor exchange only occurs through a single face of the material. Specimens are weighed intermittently during the test. Thermal conductivity is measured using a heat flow meter – Hot Disk TPS 1500. Bulk density of the specimen is calculated by measuring the volume and mass. Compressive strength is determined by loading the specimens under displacement control until failure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concept for comprehensive reuse of pyrolysis char for building materials
ce:section.ce:para: The conversion of raw coal is proceeded as illustrated in Fig. 1 , and a series of products are generated including light volatiles, liquid oils, pyrolysis chars and char ash. The innovative applications of coal and coal-derived materials are summarized in Table 1 . A wide range of carbon-based materials such as carbon fiber, carbon foam and graphene/carbon dots have been generated from coal and coal-derived products.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, a clean production technique of coal conversion starts from the organic solvent extraction using tetralin at 360 °C and 250 psi for 3 h to obtain light volatiles and coal extracted residue (CER). The light volatiles could be used as chemical feedstocks after effective separation routes. Through the pyrolysis of CER at a range of temperatures under nitrogen atmosphere, liquid oil and pyrolysis char are generated. The liquid oil is collected with ethanol solvent and analyzed via GC-MS, shown in Fig. 2 a. It clearly demonstrates that the compositions in the liquid oil become more and more complex as the pyrolysis temperature increases from 500 °C to 900 °C. This is because highly volatile compounds are decomposed from CER samples at high temperatures. The major peaks are identified by mass spectrum (NIST library) and assigned to the organic compounds, shown in Table 2 . Notably, the liquid oil mainly contains aromatics such as naphthalene and phenolic species instead of hydrocarbons since the light aliphatic component is extracted using tetralin prior the pyrolysis. The bond vibrations assigned to aromatics can also be observed with qualitative FTIR analysis in Fig. 2b. The difference of the spectrum between each sample is not obvious, indicative of same dominant compositions in liquid oils. The peaks at around 3300 cm−1 and 2910 cm−1 ascribed to stretching OH bond and stretching aliphatic CH bond are likely due to the existence of small amounts of ethanol that is for collecting liquid oil and is not removed completely (see FTIR spectrum of ethanol in Fig. 2b-inset). The ample organic matters in the liquid oil imply that the oil could be utilized as alternative fuel after the upgradation for substitution of conventional energy source and as a feedstock for chemical production like light volatile portion (Bridgwater, 2012; Jahirul et al., 2012; Lam and Chase, 2012). Owing to the polymerization feature under high temperature, the liquid oil could also act as an environmentally sustainable and economically viable carbon source for the preparation of carbonaceous materials (Jiang et al., 2015).

Pyrolysis char is the main focus of this work. Owing to the carbon-rich nature of pyrolysis char, carbothermal reactions of quartz, calcium oxide, and kaolinite with coal char under high temperature (normally above 1000 °C) have been investigated in order to generate valuable ceramics such as silicon carbide and alumina, accompanying with the evolution of CO (Wang et al., 2000, 2001). As discussed in Section 1, the unique properties including considerable specific surface area and pore channel allow pyrolysis char to perform as starting material for the preparation of insulating building materials. The product including char clay plaster, and char brick exhibits unique features (moisture adjustment and improved insulation) in comparison to conventional counterpart. It should be pointed out that the porous feature of pyrolysis char could dramatically increase the sound absorption because the sound energy dissipates with the interconnected pore networks in the concrete created by the addition of pyrolysis char, inspired by a recent literature stating that utilization of biochar as a concrete filler leads to a high sound absorption coefficient of concrete across the range of 200–2000 Hz (Cuthbertson et al., 2019). Also, the porosity feature in the concrete may be beneficial to the absorption of electromagnetic radiation. These properties would be investigated in further study. Contributed from this fabrication method, the building materials could be recycled at the end of its life for soil amendment rather than landfilling. Instead of separating or collecting carbon element from fired power plant and storing it underground meaning carbon capture and storage, the carbon species in pyrolysis char is stored and functioned in the building of a future “carbon-city”. The environmental issues could be offset by the reduction of CO2 emission in the atmosphere. The detailed study of CCP and CB specimen will be discussed in next section.

Furthermore, the pyrolysis char is calcined at 1000 °C in air (Shown in Fig. 4 b) to generate 10.90 wt % of coal ash based on the total weight of pyrolysis char, which is utilized for the fabrication of insulating foam glass owing to the high percentage of silica and alumina contents in coal ash. The chemical compositions of coal ash shown at Table 3 presents that the coal ash contains 24.02% silica, 26.18% alumina and other oxides, such as Fe2O3, CaO, MgO, etc. Despite the concentrations of rare earth elements are not monitored in this work, it is worthwhile to mention that coal/coal ash has become an important alternative resource of rare earth elements due to highly global demand and remarkable concentrations (Dai et al., 2016; Franus et al., 2015). More recently the technique on the detection and the recovery of rare earth elements in a high efficiency from coal ashes is being developed (Kose Mutlu et al., 2018; Phuoc et al., 2016; Ponou et al., 2016; Taggart et al., 2018). Fig. 3a illustrates the schematic overview of preparation process for the coal ash-based foam glass. SEM image (Fig. 3b) and typical XRD pattern (Fig. 3c) of the coal ash shows that coal ash mainly contains four phases: vitreous beads, ferrite, mullite, and amorphous carbon, within which the mullite and ferrite are crystals while beads and carbon are amorphous. There are many spherical and smooth vitreous beads with different diameters, which are formed during rapid cooling and they compose of majority of the composition within coal ash. The peaks corresponding to mullite and quartz are present via the XRD patterns, therefore it can be inferred that the alumina silicate can be assigned to mullite phase, the crystalline mullite and quartz are thermally stable. Foam glasses with different ratios of foaming agent (2% and 4%) are prepared by keeping other components unchanged, they are labeled as FA-2 and FA-4, respectively with the optical photos shown in Fig. 3d. The density of the sample decreases (from 0.189 to 0.162 g cm−3) with the increasing of foam agent content, but its pore structure tends to be non-uniform, and the strength of FA-4 is significantly reduced. The thermal insulation performance of sample is tested by Hot Disk-1500 thermal constant analyzer, and the thermal conductivity of FA-2 and FA-4 are 0.108 and 0.142 W m−1 K−1, respectively. The total porosity of FA-2 and FA-4 are 92.50% and 93.60% calculated from the ratio of bulk density and powder density.

Hence, the entire process of converting the coals into a series of highly valuable material products including light volatiles, liquid oil, CCP, CB and FA have been presented. The building materials in this work not only possess remarkable mechanical and physical properties, but also have potential economic benefits by using pyrolysis chars. As one of by-products from coal refinery, the break-even price of the char is far less than that of its competitor, leading to the decrease in the cost of building material. With the economic estimation, it shows that the cost for a ton of char clay plaster, char brick, and foam glass is reduced by ∼10%, ∼18%, ∼50%, respectively. This cost-effective and sustainable approach for new insulating building materials shows promising potentials for building applications. These outputs can significantly improve the coal economy rather than simply combustion and this method may also be functioned in the utilization of other resources such as crude oil or biomass.

Due to the potentially changing material complexity of coal, each composition may behave differently. The decomposition or evaporation of volatiles during the heating process leads to significant changes in physical and chemical features of solid residues – pyrolysis chars(Jayaraman et al., 2015). Hence, the pyrolysis char is prepared at an elevated temperature from 500 °C to 900 °C, subsequently the characterization of these samples is carried out to determine the optimal pyrolysis temperature for producing char samples for better performance in building materials. The pyrolysis char sample is named along with its corresponding pyrolysis temperature. For instance, PC-500 sample is pyrolyzed at 500 °C. Thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. 4b for CER sample) demonstrates the change in structural features of the sample during the heating process. The decomposition process of organic compounds is monitored during the pyrolysis. There is a significant decrease at around 360 °C, indicating a large quantity of organic compounds decomposed and/or evaporated at this temperature range. Ultimate analysis is to identify the elemental composition and the ratio of each element, and is summarized in Table 4 , meanwhile the ash composition obtained from calcination of CER sample at 1000 °C in air is shown in Table 3 (Jia et al., 2016; Moghtaderi et al., 2004). The pristine CER sample and its pyrolysis char sample is characterized by various techniques to decide the optimal pyrolysis temperature for the preparation of char sample.

The sample yield after pyrolysis is shown in Fig. 4a, which is an important factor that should be considered when evaluating the economic cost of the material in comparison to commercial building material. The solid residue yield decreases from 83.4% to 61.3% as the increase in pyrolysis temperature from 500 °C to 900 °C. Owing to the removal of low volatile organic compounds by solvent extraction, PC-500 and PC-600 has a reasonable high yield of 83.4% and 82.2%, respectively, supporting that the chemical property of these samples may be close to that of CER sample. A significant decrease step is observed on the chart as increasing the temperature to 700 °C, resulting from the decomposition of numerous organic matters. The yield of 61.3% is achieved when the coal sample derived from 900 °C.

The graphitic structure of CER sample and its pyrolysis char is characterized by XRD as shown in Fig. 5 a. An intensive broad peak corresponding to the (0 0 2) graphitic basal plane reflection can be observed at 2θ values between 20° to 30°, as well as weak broad peak at around 43° attributed to (1 0 0) crystal faces reflection (Jiao et al., 2015a), demonstrating the graphitic crystalline feature of the sample. The intensity of the peak at around 43° increases with increasing pyrolysis temperature, indicating that the carbon species in the sample intended to be in a stable aromatic form. Besides, there are many sharp diffraction peaks due to the minerals in the sample and these peaks became more and more intensive as the increase of pyrolysis temperature, resulting from the increased ratio of oxides. Due to the low percentage (10–15%) of inorganic minerals in the coal and char sample, the assigned peak is not dominant in XRD patterns except for two sharp and intensive peaks at 21° and 26° attributed to the silica.

FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 5b provide valuable information of the surface information of char samples, and major peaks and corresponding functional groups are listed. Through raising the pyrolysis temperature from 500 °C to 900 °C, many of vibrational bonds disappeared and the intensity of the bands declined, supporting the decomposition or evaporation of organic matters in the material (Odeh, 2015). This could also be reflected from XRD results. The weak and broad absorption band at 3300 cm−1 is attributed to the hydroxyl groups in the organic components which mainly consisted of phenolic OH or alkyl hydroxyls (Jiao et al., 2015b; Odeh, 2015). The aliphatic CH stretching is attributed to the wavenumber of around 2910 cm−1, and 1430 cm−1 corresponded to the aliphatic CH bending (Jiao et al., 2015b). The spectra of PC-500 and PC-600 is close to that of CER sample, suggesting the functional groups in these samples did not significantly change after the pyrolysis. As the pyrolysis temperature increases from 700 °C to 900 °C, weakening or disappearance of the assigned peaks implies that the organic matter in PC-700, PC-800 and PC-900 is mainly decomposed and the carbon turned to be more graphitic-like, whereas CER, PC-500, PC-600 had ample oxygenated functional groups on the surface of the material.

BET specific surface area of the samples is given in Fig. 6 . The CER sample possesses a considerably high BET surface area with the value of 370 m2 g−1, benefited from the extraction of low volatile organic matter from coal, leading to the cavity in the sample. The decreased BET specific surface area for PC-500 and PC-600 sample contributes from the organic matter melted at 500 °C but do not decompose until 700 °C, which is in agreement with previous research (Lu et al., 1999). The remaining organic matter therefore results in partial pore blockage. The yield of the pyrolysis char in Fig. 4a also indicates that there is only ∼18% weight loss when pyrolysis occurred at temperatures below 600 °C. As the temperature increases above 700 °C, the pores reappear and result in a larger specific surface area for PC-700, PC-800 and PC-900 samples. However, the BET specific surface area value is still lower than that of CER sample, which likely attributes to the residual carbons inside the pores originated from the decomposition of melted organic species above 700 °C in inert atmosphere. On the other hand, it has also been reported that collapse and/or shrinkage of carbon structure under high temperature may also lead to the lower BET specific surface area value (Bongers et al., 2000; Qi et al., 2004).

Although the CER sample possesses a higher degree of porosity than corresponding pyrolysis material, numerous types of organic functional groups remain in CER sample explained in above discussions, as well as PC-500 and PC-600 sample. This may result in the instability and efflorescence effect in air and moisture environment if these samples are utilized for the fabrication of building materials (Li et al., 2006; Spokas, 2014). In Spokas's review paper, it was investigated that there was an overall relationship in char stability as a function of the molar ratio of oxygen to carbon in the resulting chars: lower O:C molar ratios resulted in a longer predicted char half-life due to lower percentage of oxygenated groups on the chars (Spokas, 2014). The pyrolysis char with an O:C molar ratio of less than 0.2 were stable and had a half-life of more than 1000 years; whereas the char possessed a half-life of 100–1000 years as the O:C molar ratio was between 0.2 and 0.6. With the calculation from Table 4, the O:C molar ratio of the resulting pyrolysis char is 0.30, 0.26, 0.14, 0.14, and 0.13, as the order of its increasing pyrolysis temperature, supporting that higher temperature char sample is more stable than lower temperature one. Besides, the volatile organic compounds in lower temperature char sample may be released continuously over time (Li et al., 2006). Thus, the char prepared in higher pyrolysis temperature could potentially be applicable as a starting material in this work due to long-term durability. A higher specific surface area of these samples provides advantages. The pyrolysis char sample pyrolyzed between 700 °C to 900 °C is therefore utilized as starting material for building product in next section to demonstrate the superior performance.

Fig. 7 illustrates the SEM microscopic images of pyrolysis char sample. The SEM images clearly show the porous feature of the pyrolysis char; however, the pore sizes are not homogeneously uniform. Obtained from the magnified SEM image, the average pore size is determined as 0.51 μm. It has been discussed that the porous microstructure results in a low bulk density and thus reducing the thermal conductivity of building material. This is due to the fact that the air contained inside the pores has a lower thermal conductivity compared to other compositions, thus hindering the heat transfer between both sides of building material. Moreover, resulting from large specific surface and high porosity, the water could be absorbed and stored in these pores, resulting in the effect of regulating indoor humidity.

Accordingly, the CCP specimen is fabricated following the procedure in Section 2.1. The subscripts in sample names (CCPx,y or CBx,y) indicate the weight ratio of sands replaced by pyrolysis chars and the temperature of pyrolysis char, respectively. Fig. 8 a demonstrated the improved percentage of water absorption and desorption capacity of char clay plasters in average of four cycles compared to that of blank plaster specimen, where the desorption capability is defined by the ratio of the water removed from the specimen in the desorption cycle to the weight of the specimen. The blank plaster had a low water capacity and the amount of absorbed/desorbed water has increased when the pyrolysis char material is introduced, which is attributed to the porosity of the char sample. Amongst the char plaster samples, CCP40,850 possessed the highest water capacity than other specimens. The water absorption and desorption capability of this specimen increases by 67.93% and 45.77%, respectively, compared to that of blank plaster specimen. Moreover, the char plaster samples derived from the char pyrolyzed at 850 °C had better water absorption performance and desorption compared to those derived from the char pyrolyzed at 750 °C. This is likely assigned to the increased porosity and larger BET specific surface area of the char sample pyrolyzed at higher temperature. Resulting from large specific surface and high porosity, the water could be absorbed and stored in these pores, resulting in the effect of regulating indoor humidity. This could be reflected in Table 5 , showing the BET specific surface area of char plaster prepared with 850 °C char material is greater than that with 750 °C char material. Besides, the BET specific surface area of blank plaster specimen is merely 34 m2 g−1, whereas the char clay plaster has a much larger surface area. This explains the fact that the pyrolysis char-derived plaster has a better performance of moisture buffering and insulation property.

Fig. 8b summarizes the thermal conductivities of blank plaster and char clay plaster specimens to demonstrate the insulation property of this building material. In comparison to the blank plaster made of sand possessing thermal conductivity of 0.6993 W m−1 K−1, the char clay plasters exhibited an enhanced insulation feature with lower thermal conductivity owing to the porous feature of pyrolysis char, as well as bulk density. The air contained inside the pores has a lower thermal conductivity compared to other compositions, thus hindering the heat transfer between both sides of building material (Gutiérrez-González et al., 2012; Serhat Başpınar and Kahraman, 2011). The CCP specimen has a much lower thermal conductivity value in comparison to blank plaster specimen depending on the preparation temperature and mass ratio of pyrolysis chars, supporting that the pyrolysis char-incorporated plaster specimen possesses an enhanced thermal insulation feature with an increase of up to 73.70% for building material use. Notably, the plaster specimen with higher pyrolysis char content performs better and exhibits lowest thermal conductivity of 0.1839 W m−1 K−1 and 0.1933 W m−1 K−1 for CCP60,750 and CCP60,850, respectively. As indicated in Fig. 9 a, a relatively strong relationship (R2 = 0.933) can be seen between thermal conductivity and bulk density in CCP samples. This phenomenon is previously observed in literature (Binici et al., 2007; Devecioğlu and Biçer, 2016).

The relationship between thermal conductivity and temperature is revealed in Fig. 8c, showing a significant drop in thermal conductivity with an increase in temperature for CCP specimen and blank specimen. This is attributed to the existence of free water and/or chemically-bonded water in the specimen and rising temperature will result in the gradually removal of the moisture in the pores, meaning that the space would be replaced with air. As such, the thermal conductivity decreases by 26.02%, 26.16% and 25.14% with increasing temperature for blank, CCP60,750 and CCP60,850 specimen, respectively. The specific heat capacity of specimen is shown in Fig. 8d as a function of temperature. In the temperature region between 25 °C and 200 °C, a significant number of heat should be provided to the removal of free water and/or hydrated water in the materials. The peak between 100 °C and 150 °C suggests that free water evaporates predominantly at this temperature range and when increasing the temperature to 200 °C, the specific heat capacity would drop. This is consistent with previous results (Othuman and Wang, 2011). Besides, it is indicated in Table 5 that the bulk density of CCP specimen is almost half the lower than that of blank specimen. As such the specific heat capacity of the CCP specimen (heat absorbed in cubic meter unit) is lower than that of blank specimen.

Fig. 10 a, Fig. 10b and Table 6 represents the physical and mechanical properties of char brick specimens. An intentional effect of incorporating these particles is a significant reduction in the bulk density of the specimen, which is attributed to the high porosity of the pyrolysis char materials. This could be backup in Fig. 6, demonstrating the BET specific surface area of pyrolysis char materials. As shown in Fig. 9b and c, the thermal conductivity of the specimen mathematically correlated to its bulk density as well as the compressive strength, which is in agreement with CCP result. When 50 wt % of sands are replaced by pyrolysis char materials (CB50,750 and CB50,850 sample), the density of the brick is reduced by 40.6%–45.9% compared to the blank brick. As a result, the corresponding thermal conductivity is reduced by 63.3–64.9%. The porosity and the reduction in density therefore improved the insulation properties of the as-fabricated char bricks with a much lower thermal conductivity for sample CB50,750 (0.5484 W m−1 K−1) and sample CB50,850 (0.5240 W m−1 K−1) compared to the reference blank material (1.4940 W m−1 K−1). When the weight ratio of pyrolysis char decreases to 30%, the compressive strength increases up to 19.1 MPa (for CB30,850 sample), meeting the building code and industrial standard of brick (Association, 2007). It should be mentioned that there is a trade-off between the mechanical property and insulation property so that thermal conductivity of the brick could be balanced by adjusting the mass ratio of pyrolysis char.

Fig. 10c demonstrates the thermal conductivity of blank, CB50,750 and CB50,850 specimen decreases by 12.85%, 19.89% and 15.59% as the temperature increasing to 200 °C, respectively. This is attributed to the removal of moisture including free water and/or chemically-bonded water in the specimen, which could be evidenced by previous literature (Kodur and Sultan, 2003; Lie and Kodur, 1996; Shin et al., 2002). However, a few studies stated a different kinetic thermal conductivity behavior of the insulating materials such as fiberglass, rock-wool, extruded polystyrene and refractory brick (Berardi and Naldi, 2017; Shimizu et al., 2013). This is due to the difference of material's inherent nature. The mentioned materials are either non-porous or fabricated under extremely high temperature so that there is not a substantial amount of free water and/or chemically-bonded water in these materials. The thermal conductivity therefore increases with increasing temperature because the thermal conductivity is strongly influenced by heat radiation and phonon conduction under these circumstances (Shimizu et al., 2013). Notably, for our specimen, when a repeated test is carried out immediately after the completion of initial thermal conductivity analysis, the thermal conductivity of CB50,750 specimen increases with increasing temperature (for RE-CB50,750 specimen in Fig. 10c) owing to the fact that the majority of moisture in the specimen has been removed. This phenomenon also supports the fact that the thermal conductivity strongly depends on the moisture content in the as-prepared specimen, and after the removal of moisture, the temperature-dependent thermal conductivity law of CB specimen is consistent with that of fiberglass, rock-wool, extruded polystyrene and refractory brick. In Fig. 10d, the specific heat capacity of CB specimen as a function of temperature is similar to that of CCP specimen, showing a peak between 100 °C and 150 °C where the moisture evaporates.

Table 7 presents the physical properties of the building materials from some literature. In comparison to these materials, the products in this study demonstrated comparable or superior thermal conductivities as well as reduced bulk densities. Despite different formulations for the building materials causing the fact that the CB specimens did not outperform existing materials in terms of thermal conductivity value, the reduction in thermal conductivity of CB is up to 65%, indicating the incorporation of pyrolysis char had a significant positive effect on the improvement of insulation property of brick specimens. In comparison to other pore-creating methods frequently involving high temperature procedure or purified reagents, the pyrolysis chars possessing remarkable specific surfaces are directly utilized to provide pores on the building materials without any modification. What's more, the low cost of pyrolysis char will make this approach more attractive and become an indispensable process in the coal conversion. It should be pointed out that the porous feature of pyrolysis char could dramatically reduce the intensity of the sound, thereby producing a final building product that is also an efficient noise absorber aside from the property.

A clean production technique of coal conversion has been investigated in this work and a series of valuable products including pyrolysis char, light volatiles, and liquid oils have been produced from raw coal via solvent extraction and high temperature pyrolysis.

Benefited from highly porosity, ample micropore channel and chemically stable feature, high-temperature char sample is then utilized as a cost-effective substitution for fabrication of building materials (char clay plaster and char brick). The results demonstrate that pyrolysis char-derived specimens display the enhanced insulation properties, the unique moisture buffering properties, and the lower bulk densities compared to blank products.

By incorporating pyrolysis char material into plaster specimens, the water absorption and desorption capability increase by 67.93% and 45.77%, respectively, indicating that the moisture could be absorbed and stored in the pores, resulting in the effect of regulating indoor humidity and able to maintain a proper humidity level. The reduction in the thermal conductivity of the CCP specimen up to 73.70% implies the material has a better insulation property compared to blank plaster specimen. Similarly, the CB specimen possesses a much better insulation property compared to brick blank specimen along with a lightweight feature (1.24–1.36 g cm−3). The thermal conductivity of CCP and CB specimen show an inverse relationship of temperature due to the existence of free water and hydrated water, which is evaporated between 100 °C and 150 °C.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project is supported by the funding from the Wyoming State Legislator through the School of Energy Resources, University of Wyoming, USA.
